data.title: Pampa Energia: The Turning Point In Argentina's Most Resilient Energy Player
data.description:
When I published my previous article on Pampa Energía (PAM) in late August, the ADR was trading at around $70, and at that time, my position was buy. The elections were still two months away, and the market was moving blindly, speculating on opposing regulatory scenarios.

In September we saw a more negative climate, especially after the victory of the opposition in the Province of Buenos Aires, a result that was reversed on 26 October, when the victory of Milei generated one of the strongest movements of the year in Argentine energy assets. At that time, the market immediately understood that it had ahead of it at least two years of a more predictable, freer, and less interventionist framework, something that could directly improve the profitability of an integrated player like PAM, to the point that in the pre-market of the day after the election, the stock advanced about 25%.

To explain the PAM trade today, which is around $92, an increase of more than 30% since my previous article, it is not enough to look only at the electoral result, which I consider very favorable.

I also think the market finally began to price a company that had been doing the work in silence for several quarters, and that ended up consolidating that perception with the Q3 results. In this scenario I reaffirm my position and still see Pampa as a buy.

Q3: the quarter where everything was aligned

When analyzing the results of Q3, I see that PAM continues moving in the direction it was already doing in previous quarters, with shale oil growing rapidly, gas holding even when demand behaves very erratically, electricity generation benefiting from deregulation, and capex that does not work as a brake, but as the bridge to a more ambitious production profile.

Without a doubt, Rincón de Aranda is the driver that most changes the story. To put it in context, three new pads in the quarter brought shale oil production to 14,400 bpd, almost triple Q2, and a seventh pad connected after the quarter brought volume to 16,000 bpd.

However, what struck me most were PAM’s production expectations for the next two years, as mentioned in the call:

We expect to exit 2025 producing 20,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day. To support further growth and leverage idle capacity, trumpa capacity in the pipeline, we plan to install an additional temporary facility next year, increasing production to an average of 28,000 barrels per day by the second half of 2026. Our target is 45,000 barrels per day by 2027 once Vaca Muerta oil Sur pipeline and our central processing facility as known as CPF are brought online.

These figures also have to be read in a context where, until recently, PAM was essentially a gas company. Today oil is no longer a secondary activity and is becoming a source of EBITDA, with margins increasing as lifting costs decline. The CFO explained that this is what is happening:

Therefore, we will see a reduction in our lifting cost for oil from $10 to around $9.1, $9.2 per barrel, and that will drive down our overall lifting cost to around $6.2 per barrel equivalent.

For me this line summarizes everything, because it shows a reduction in costs that does not arise from accounting adjustments, but from real scale, supported by volumes that grow steadily.

Although the results of the shale were extraordinary, they were not accompanied by a deterioration in gas; on the contrary, winter allowed the company to demonstrate the technical capacity of PAM: 17.6MMm3/day, the highest value in the history of the company.

Electricity generation also performed well, even with a 9% drop due to maintenance. PEPE6 contributed more than expected, and the progress of deregulation since Milei’s assumption is beginning to be seen, as PAM historically operated with margins "tied" to the regulation in generation. Today the story is different, and the possibility of self-sufficiency in gas begins to change the dynamics of margins.

Finally, capex. Pampa invested $317MM in the quarter, +225% YoY, and still maintained a cash position of $881MM and a net debt of $874MM, with a leverage of 1.3x.

We know that 2025 and 2026 are the most intensive years in terms of investment for Rincón de Aranda and projects associated with generation, as explained in detail by the CFO:

This year is basically what we projected on the budget or slightly lower than what we projected on the budget because of very small delays in the deployment on some payments that are not going to be done this year, but are going to be done early next year. So it’s going to be around $1.1 billion. And next year, we expect roughly to maintain that level of CapEx. That is basically until we finish the ramp-up of Rincón de Aranda because -- as we said, Rincón de Aranda takes 70%, 75% of that number.

Valuation and peers: why PAM remains cheap even after rising 30%?

Every time I go back to analyze the valuation of PAM I find myself facing the same dilemma, since it is an integrated company that lives in several worlds at once. It’s not just upstream, it’s not just gas, and it’s not just generation. Even so, I see that there are numbers that repeat and show that the stock continues with a discount difficult to justify.

Today PAM is trading at an EV/EBITDA FWD close to 6x, which implies a discount of around 40% compared to the sector average, and the P/E FWD is around 14x, around 25% below the sector. This is especially striking for a company projecting EPS growth far above the industry.

To put it in context, if I look at Vista (VIST), which is the most visible peer in unconventional Argentine oil, I see that it operates with an EV/EBITDA close to 4.8x, and YPF (YPF) is even slightly above that, around 4.8x as well.

But we must also bear in mind that the comparison is not entirely fair, as PAM has lower risk, less exposure to upstream regulation, and greater diversification. I also note that PAM now has more accelerated growth, as VIST is now entering a more moderate stage after the strong growth of the previous two years, while, as shown in Q3, PAM is just entering its phase of accelerated expansion.

Growing, however, I see that the gap is even more marked. PAM projects a revenue increase of over 50% YoY for the next twelve months, while the sector is in a low digit. The FWD EBITDA grows around 15%, above average, and the EBIT is practically multiplying compared to the figures of the sector. The EPS has an estimated increase of more than 100%, a magnitude that allows me to explain why I consider it difficult to maintain the current multiples.

In profitability, PAM also stands out. The ROA is close to 5%, about twice the industry average, and the net margin is around 17%, above 12% of the sector. However, I also see a squeezed operating margin, but this is explained by what we analyzed previously, a context of high capex, in the transition to shale oil. With lifting costs down to the PAM estimate of $6/boe and production that will expand strongly over the next 24 months, we can expect an improvement in efficiency.

Risks

After reviewing the quarter and valuation, I am also clear that the risks exist and are not minor. First of all, despite the results favorable to the current administration, we must remember that Argentina remains a volatile country, even in a more liberalized context like the one we have today.

On the other hand, the projected capex for 2025 and 2026 is demanding, which forces PAM to have a very precise execution, since the speed at which it invests does not leave too much margin of error.

At the same time, PAM remains exposed to the Brent movement, although the use of hedges helps moderate extremes. We must also consider that the Chilean market, which today provides a significant volume of gas exports, can vary according to conditions that change every year, so it is not appropriate to assume that this flow will be totally stable.

Conclusion

When I look at the quarter’s results in the current macroeconomic context, where the Milei government will seek to advance market-oriented reforms, I see PAM entering a phase that is clearly different. Today shale oil is reconfiguring its EBITDA structure at a speed that a year ago seemed unlikely to me, gas continues to show stability, and electricity generation finally finds a regulatory framework that allows it to capture more margin.

And all this happens while the stock continues to trade with multiples that do not reflect the magnitude or the speed of growth projected for the next two years. That is why, as in August, I maintain my position: Buy.

data.title: The Trade Desk: A Generational Buying Opportunity Emerges (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Back in April of this year, I initiated coverage on The Trade Desk, Inc. (TTD) with a technically based piece. The stock did gain for a period after my article, but after pulling back significantly, losses now total ~20% since publication. With consumer confidence seemingly shaky, it's actually not a major surprise that there has been considerable weakness, as the willingness to pay for ads may be under some pressure at the moment. With the company reporting earnings back in November, today let's see if the current stock price presents an opportunity for investors.

In the below analysis, it is determined that not only is there opportunity in the stock, there is likely currently a generational opportunity. While revenue growth slowed again for Q3 and guidance for Q4 was softer than desired, the long-term growth story remains unshaken. The company is a leader in this space and has the track record to prove it. Furthermore, the digital ads market continues to expand, and so if you look past the near term consumer confidence headwinds, the rock-bottom level in the valuation is a major buying opportunity. In my previous article, I mainly based my bearishness off of the technicals, but after reviewing the company's fundamentals today, I believe The Trade Desk is a strong buy at current levels.

Big Picture Should Calm Investors

Above is a timeline of the company that's in their 2025 Q3 earnings presentation. I'm not going to get into the details of the numbers. Instead, I believe this infographic provides a good big picture sense of where things are really at. Sure, 2025 has been a year to forget in terms of stock performance, but really it's more than likely that temporary ad spending headwinds will eventually dissipate. The progress they have made throughout the years speaks to their commitment to growth, and so while they are hitting some speed bumps now, odds are that the company will bounce back.

Above, we discussed that history shows the company's internals are strong. Well, let's take a look forward. Despite consumer weakness potentially weighing on ad spend now, the long-term trend is that the digital ad market is only getting bigger. The market is truly enormous at $935 billion+, and so the potential for growth is immense as the industry as a whole continues to grow. When combining the strong internals of the company with the long-term trends of market expansion, I would say that the outlook for The Trade Desk is quite clearly a bright one. Now, let's turn towards how they performed in Q3.

Growth Slows For 2nd Straight Quarter

For 2025 Q3, The Trade Desk reported total revenues of $739 billion, representing YoY growth of 18%. While these figures are really respectable in isolation, note that the growth rate has actually hit multiyear lows for two consecutive quarters. Back in Q2, there was a top line increase of 19%, and so there has been a modest deceleration for Q3. It is more than likely that consumer weakness is a driver of this relative sluggishness. However, as mentioned earlier, this will eventually pass.

Despite the lowest growth in years, their revenues were still able to beat analyst expectations by $20.09 million, and so results are better than feared. Furthermore, the company reported that they had customer retention of over 95% in the quarter. They also stated that this metric has been this high for 11 consecutive years, and so it is obvious that their platform remains the go-to for ad buyers. This is another source of reassurance that there is nothing specifically wrong with the company's value proposition.

EBITDA Margin Expands

Firstly, for the bottom line, The Trade Desk reported an adjusted EPS of $0.45, up 9.76% YoY. While that is actually a rebound from the previous quarter's sluggish growth rate, this increase still lags the top line growth rate as an indication of shaky profitability. However, this may be a bit misleading regarding the true efficiency of the business.

The company likes to use adjusted EBITDA as one of their core metrics, and I agree that this figure usually tells the better story vis-a-vis the actual profitability of a company's operations. For adjusted EBITDA, they reported $317 million, up 23% YoY. That outpaces the revenue growth rate. As a result, adjusted EBITDA margin expanded from 41% to 43% as a demonstration of operational improvement. Therefore, while the company can't control the macro-environment, they continue to make internal improvements that count.

Share Buybacks Are Aggressive

In Q3, The Trade Desk generated operating cash flows of $224.69 million, which is a little disappointing, as that represents a drop YoY. After subtracting CAPEX and whatnot, FCFs came out to being $155 million, and that also represents a notable drop from the year ago quarter. Personally, this isn't too concerning for me, as the long-term outlook remains strong, as emphasized many times already. What I really want to discuss in this section is their aggressive share buyback policy.

In the quarter, The Trade Desk bought back $310 million worth of Class A common stock. That left $60 million in remaining share repurchase authorization, and they used it all in October. Firstly, note that they bought back far more stock than they had FCFs in the quarter. In fact, the $310 million is more than FCF for any quarter in the past three years, and so it is likely they see their stock as significantly undervalued. It seems that they are tapping into their cash reserves, and this can be seen as a statement of confidence regarding future cash flows. Looking forward, the board has already approved another $500 million for their share repurchase program, and so shareholders should be encouraged by what they see.

Near Term Outlook

For the fourth quarter, The Trade Desk sees revenues of at least $840 million. That means that they're expecting top line growth of at least 13% YoY. Since there was an 18% revenue increase in Q3, there is a good likelihood that there will be a third straight quarter of deceleration, and that probably played a part in spooking investors.

As for adjusted EBITDA, they are projecting a figure of $375 million, and that implies a YoY growth rate of 7%. That lags the projected top line growth rate, and so the strong operating profitability we saw in Q3 is not expected to continue.

Overall, I would say that Q4 guidance is indeed weaker than desired. While the slowdown in revenue growth is not too surprising with consumer confidence still under pressure, I would say the weak adjusted EBITDA estimate is a bit disappointing.

Still, this is just a near-term outlook, and I view the long term growth story as still intact despite these speed bumps. Furthermore, you can see in the EPS chart from back in the adjusted EBITDA discussion that EPS growth is expected to bounce back as we head into 2026 and so Q4 weakness will likely be the exception rather than the norm even in the near future.

Valuation Hits Multiyear Low

As you can see above, the forward P/E ratio for The Trade Desk has collapsed this year. After a brief rebound during the middle of the year, the multiple now finds itself at fresh multiyear lows. I believe this potentially creates a generational buying opportunity.

In today's article, we have learned that the company is indeed facing some near-term headwinds. Revenue growth is slowing, and despite strong EBITDA margin in Q3, there will be profitability weakness in Q4. However, I believe this is really all just noise. The key takeaway is that the company has a track record of growth, and the long term outlook for digital ads is as bright as ever. Near-term consumer headwinds are here and now, but they won't last forever. Once the environment improves, I believe The Trade Desk is very well positioned to bounce right back.

There is a risk that the consumer slump will last longer than expected, with the direction of inflation still being uncertain, but sooner or later, the wind will be back in the company's sails. Therefore, I believe the multiyear low in the forward P/E ratio really creates a prime entry opportunity for investors.

Final Thoughts

A generational buying opportunity usually involves a fundamentally robust business selling for a major relative discount as a result of temporary headwinds causing investor panic. I believe this sums up the situation that The Trade Desk is in right now. Especially when considering that the company is now aggressively innovating in AI to minimize disruption potential, the outlook for the business is strong, as near-term headwinds are unable to reverse positive long term trends for digital ads. As stated at the beginning, my previous article on The Trade Desk was rooted in the technicals, but after looking at the fundamental setup today, I believe the stock is a strong buy currently.

data.title: U.S. Equities: I Want To Put All My Eggs In This Basket
data.description:
My first rating for the Invesco QQQ ETF (QQQ) was a hold. It has been almost a year, and since then the total return has been 19.8%. My sentiment at the time was basically similar for the S&P500 (SPY) (IVV) as well, a secular investment but one that deserved a cautious stance given the valuation and eventual risk of drawdown.

If we look at it tactically, I believe that both still deserve a cautious buy rating, while some emerging market ETFs may be more attractive given the momentum, a possible rotation, and other factors. I even mentioned the importance of having cash in the last podcast I participated in.

But in this article, I don't want to talk about the cautious stance or tactical trading, but rather what I've been thinking about for some time. I am Brazilian, and therefore I end up investing part of my money in Brazil, both in Brazilian Treasury (high-interest rate) and in the stock market, because I know some assets well, I feel the need to remove this home bias and buy more American/global assets. There are several reasons for this, but mainly because of what is being called American exceptionalism.

As Buffett says, you can't bet against America. I can't pinpoint just one reason why the US is what it is, but in the end, what matters is that it is a country where innovation has evolved in a way that has not occurred in any other country, and this environment has been very favorable for the creation of value through companies, which has obviously had a huge impact on the index.

Why Is The United States Exceptional?

Drawing on my bachelor's degree in Economics/International Affairs, I can think of several reasons why the United States is an exceptional country. Geography is a very strong point, with self-sufficiency in several important resources, from food to energy, access to the ocean, being a large country, and so on.

But even so, some other countries also have these characteristics, including Brazil, which is also (at least in theory) a liberal democracy. Still on the political side, the United States, in my view, stands out for having strong institutions.

I know that perhaps, for you who are American and reading this, American politics may seem very troubled, and lately it has indeed become more complex, but this does not even compare to emerging markets. Here, in just a few years, the country can completely change direction just because an opposition president took office and wanted to change various policies, initiatives, and laws with a few “signatures,” unlike in the US, where everything is much more solid and reliable.

But even so, I don't think only politics and geography explain why the United States has been so unique in recent decades. I believe it is something that transcends the more tangible factors and enters into intangible factors, such as culture, innovation, being a country with many risk takers, which in the end becomes a very vibrant environment for capitalism. Think about AI, robotics, or more specifically, Nvidia (NVDA), where an entrepreneur founded an extremely innovative company that, after years and years of developing different products and investing heavily in R&D, became the largest company in the world. This directly impacts stock returns and earnings growth in the S&P 500.

Doing this in other markets, such as Brazil, is not impossible, but we have far fewer examples. Similarly, we have far fewer “Warren Buffetts” in Brazil. In other words, investing in America has been synonymous with excellent compounding through entrepreneurship and innovation, which ultimately meant high shareholder value.

In the Long Term, US Equities Are Better, Parallel to Emerging Markets

Drawing a parallel with emerging markets, an unexpected electoral change may not only mean a few years of mediocre government, but may also lead to structural changes in the markets, such as being negatively impacted by tariffs, having the currency drastically devalued, interest rates doubling in a short time frame, and all of this greatly impacting the company's earnings and valuations. In other words, there is a potential for negative surprise that is totally a game changer.

Itaú Unibanco (ITUB) is one of the great examples of the Brazilian stock market. It is a bank with very consistent return on equity, very good execution for decades, and which has been able to generate good returns in recent years. But even so, it is nothing out of this world compared to American stocks. In BRL, Itaú's diluted EPS was R$2.36 in 2015 and R$3.78 in 2024. But in dollars, Itaú Unibanco's EPS was $0.59 in 2015 and $0.61 in 2024, reflecting a virtually structural currency devaluation. More recently, the exchange rate has been favorable for Brazil, with a devaluation of the USD around the world, so Itaú's EPS in recent months has been ~$0.77 over the last 12 months. Still, this is an excellent example of how even good companies, with good execution and visionary management that knew how to adapt, were unable to reward shareholders well in dollar terms.

The lack of change is also clear over time, and we don't even need to go that far. 2010 was “only” 15 years ago, but it was enough for the S&P 500 to change dramatically. In 2010, although we already saw names like Apple (AAPL) and Microsoft (MSFT), we also saw players like Exxon Mobil (XOM), Walmart (WMT), Chevron (CVX), Procter & Gamble (PG), and so on. Today, as you probably already know, the top 1 is Nvidia, which was not even in the top 20 at the time, and today, companies like Apple, Microsoft, Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG) that were already in the index have a market cap many times that of that time, because earnings have risen sharply and companies have reinvented themselves. The Apple of 2010 is very different from the Apple of 2025. Now AAPL is more consolidated, services play a much more important role in revenue growth and margins, Alphabet is much more diversified, and so on.

Now, for the Brazilian index (EWZ), in 2010 the main ones were Petrobras (PBR), Vale (VALE), and Itaú Unibanco. Now, if we consider the Ibovespa, the largest is Vale, the second largest is Petrobras, and the third largest is Itaú. But if we consider the EWZ, we have something new. Nu Holdings (NU) now appears in first place (followed by the same names mentioned above). For me, this is a double-edged sword.

While it shows that there were Brazilian players who truly innovated and managed to grow significantly, even disrupting an industry and expanding internationally, it also shows me two things. The first is that companies like Nu Holdings were the exception. The second is that even though it is Brazilian, Nu Holdings is listed in the United States and receives attention from large global institutional investors, including Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A)(BRK.B) at one point, so they also rely on U.S. markets indirectly.

This parallel is not to say that Brazil does not have good companies or anything like that. There are good companies that have managed to generate shareholder value, as well as potential trades to be made, especially in positive cycles, both in macroeconomic and political cycles, as well as in commodity cycles. But it is clear where I want my money to be when we are talking about decades, especially being a value, but also a growth investor.

Personally, I invest for the very long term. I am willing to hold assets for decades and reap big rewards in 2050 or something like that. I believe that if I do this by increasingly exposing myself to US assets that have high potential, it could be truly exponential. If 20 years ago you asked the average investor what the main technologies or trends of 2025 would be, it would have been virtually impossible to predict, and even though we have hints that robotics, AI, and perhaps some technologies that are still in their infancy, such as virtual reality (VR), could have a major impact on society, at the same time we are also talking about technologies that often have not even been created yet. And if I had to bet on which country, or rather, which country's companies will dominate future technologies, I would bet on the major American companies and also on some incumbents.

SPY And QQQ As A Hedge

This is also why, even though I am a stock picker, I want to start a position in SPY. I imagine that the world's leading robotics company will be Amazon. At the same time, I cannot be sure of this. Furthermore, it is difficult to know whether companies such as Nvidia will continue to have such an important presence in society over the next 20 or 30 years, or whether something new will take its place. Therefore, even though I prefer to choose which company to buy, I will also eventually start a position in ETFs, whether in indices linked to the S&P 500 or the Nasdaq.

This is a role that I see many people overlook. In addition to providing diversification, this type of asset can also be seen as a type of hedge, since perhaps the valuation of one or two companies you are buying now may not bear the expected fruit, or even one may work out but another may disappear from the map, but on average, these ETFs have delivered solid and consistent returns, so I think it is structurally worthwhile to have a portion of the portfolio linked to this.

Bonus: Why Not Other Developed Countries?

Well, you may be asking yourself, “OK, I understand your point, but what about other countries like Germany or Japan?” And as much as these are also countries with a good culture, strong institutions, and more stable currencies than those of emerging markets, the U.S. still has another very strong advantage over them, which is demographics. The fertility rate in the U.S. is not the best, at 1.84 children per woman, while the “ideal” is 2, but the outlook is better than most of its peers. In Japan (EWJ), the number is 1.4 births per woman, which is very low, Germany (EWG) is at 1.6, and Italy (EWI) is at 1.26.

In addition, with the potential for immigration to the US, this also balances population growth somewhat if we think about the coming decades. The implications of this are numerous. Countries with a faster aging population and fewer young people end up suffering in various sectors, such as real estate and consumption, not to mention the economic implications related to retirement.

In short, this is another positive point for U.S. equities when we think about the long term.

Bottom Line: For Now, Cautious Buy Rating

As I mentioned throughout the article, I increasingly want to migrate and have a larger position in U.S. equities, almost as if I want to have all my eggs in this basket, and structurally, I consider these assets a buy rating. At the same time, I am a little more cautious at the moment.

As I mentioned in my article on the 2026 outlook, for the short term (next quarters), I do not believe much in a new multiple expansion, but rather in a healthy contraction. So even though I am structurally optimistic and also believe that companies will deliver another solid earnings growth, I have some reservations about the valuation decline of companies such as Apple and Palantir (PLTR). Therefore, my rating for U.S. equities would be what I call a “cautious buy.” I continue to buy, but more selectively, and also accumulate cash.

data.title: QDVO Vs. JEPQ: Why I Am Rotating To Capture The Nasdaq Upside
data.description:
Investment thesis

Many investors who are focused on generating high monthly income with less volatility than the broader market (S&P 500 and Nasdaq-100) are interested in choosing assets whose strategy is linked to covered options. Among these are the Amplify CWP Growth & Income ETF (QDVO) and the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ). In my opinion, they are an ideal pair, where quality contrasts with quantity (yield). However, while QDVO wins in terms of total return in a growing market, the JEPQ wins in terms of monthly yield in a sideways market. This is because JEPQ uses synthetic options and hedges most of its portfolio, often limiting price growth in a strong bull market. The QDVO takes a different approach, using tactical covered calls in an effort not to limit the growth of the strongest stocks.

Ultimately, with market prices showing strong growth, QDVO emerges as the winner in its battle with JEPQ. Therefore, I am selecting one of these ETFs as an asset that is recommended as a “buy” right now. And that is QDVO. As for JEPQ, my previous “Buy” rating has been revised to “Hold.” This is simply because I want to focus my new purchases exclusively on a fund that offers me the potential to capitalize on the growth of the Nasdaq-100 index in the foreseeable future. Judging by the latest news, markets are beginning to shake off fears caused by the possible end of the AI boom.

Fundamental similarities between QDVO and JEPQ strategies and their main differences

The distinctive feature of JEPQ is that it is linked to out-of-the-money call options. Not only does the fund own shares included in the Nasdaq-100 index, it also sells short-term call options on the index itself. These provide premium income in the short term and enable monthly payments to investors. The main concentration of assets is focused on Mag-7 stocks, making this fund dependent on the price dynamics of the world's largest technology companies. To generate regular monthly income distributed among ETF investors, options are written, but despite creating potential for short-term income, these create an additional barrier to long-term growth. This usually means that JEPQ's returns will be lower than the Nasdaq-100 benchmark.

Within the framework of QDVO, its investment strategy is structured such that, in normal circumstances, it invests at least 80% of its net assets in growth-oriented US exchange-traded securities and employs an “option strategy” consisting of selling US exchange-traded call options on such equity securities. Actively managing the fund lets ETF managers spot opportunities and risks, as well as make decisions in real time. The covered call options and dividends help reduce volatility relative to the overall market during periods of widespread market decline. Similar to JEPQ, their assets are mainly concentrated in Mag-7 stocks, making this fund dependent on the price movements of the largest technology companies.

Despite the fact that these funds have very similar investment strategies (only at first glance), a deeper analysis of the fundamental similarities between QDVO and JEPQ reveals four main points that distinguish them. These are the stock selection strategy, the type of option strategy, the approach to “growth capture,” and profitability.

Point #1. Stock selection strategy.

The main difference between QDVO and JEPQ is that one fund tries to focus on picking 20-30 top growth stocks (even though the portfolio itself has 52 assets right now) to make up most of the portfolio, whereas JEPQ goes for more diversification, choosing 80-90 assets (the portfolio currently contains 107 assets). The latter is due to the fact that the latter fund uses JPMorgan's proprietary model. Thanks to this, the JEPQ portfolio is very similar to the structure of the Nasdaq-100 index, with minor deviations in the share of each security. As a result, the concentration of capital in the 10 largest assets in the QDVO portfolio is as high as 62.64%, while for JEPQ it is 46.76% (which is also a fairly high concentration of risk).

Point #2. Choosing an option strategy.

I think this is where the main difference between QDVO and JEPQ comes in. As I mentioned, JEPQ is a fund that sells options not on individual stocks in its portfolio, but rather on the Nasdaq-100 index as a whole. It does this through synthetic ELN options. The essence of these options is that fund managers sell the growth “ceiling” for the entire index at once. The transaction is repeated every month, regardless of the price change. This is done regardless of whether there has been growth or not. QDVO uses a strategy of covering calls on stocks that is more understandable to the market. The fund managers sell call options on individual stocks depending on market volatility. This means that these options will not necessarily be sold for all stocks. Portfolio manager Kevin O'Brien sells options that the team believes are associated with overbought stocks. Often, these sales occur during periods of sharp price volatility.

Point #3. The “growth capture” function.

Like I said in the intro, QDVO is an ETF that lets you tap into the full growth potential of the market. Options only cover part of the portfolio, and even then, not always. That’s why strong price growth in the market doesn’t limit the growth of the capital base. Speaking of JEPQ, their strategy of selling options on the entire Nasdaq-100 index limits capital growth in the presence of a sharp bull market. If the Nasdaq price increases by 10% in a month, for example, JEPQ's growth may be around 6-7%. The reason for this is that the remaining percentage of growth is eaten up by the options sold.

Point #4. Yield.

JEPQ historically has an advantage in this regard, because it traditionally demonstrates higher yields for its investors than QDVO. Since the fund sells options on its entire portfolio, it creates the opportunity to earn higher yields. With the QDVO portfolio, the sale of options is selective, meaning that the yield is lower. This fund's managers offer a compromise solution in the form of future capital growth in exchange for higher returns from payments. Recently, however, dividend yield figures have evened out. Currently, QDVO's Dividend Yield (TTM) is higher at 9.84%, compared to JEPQ's 9.47%. Furthermore, QDVO's Total Return for the last year was 17.95%, compared to JEPQ's 14.08%.

Comparative table of QDVO and JEPQ fund characteristics

The table below provides a brief comparison of the main criteria, which allows you to determine the difference between QDVO and JEPQ.

Criteria

QDVO

JEPQ

Management Type

Active. Managers manually select stocks and decide when to sell options

Active/Systematic. Stock selection is active, but option selling is systematic and ongoing

Portfolio Structure

Concentrated Portfolio. 52 select growth stocks, focusing on the "best ideas"

Broad Portfolio. 107 stocks, attempting to replicate the Nasdaq-100 index structure with minor deviations

Assets in Top 10

62.64%

46.76%

Option Strategy

Selling covered calls on individual stocks; covering selected positions rather than the entire portfolio

Selling options on the Nasdaq-100 index (via ELN), covering a significant portion of the portfolio

Upside Limit

Risks of the investment thesis

Since I am the type of investor who believes in the continuation of the bullish rally in the US high-tech sector, my goal is to generate above-market returns without sacrificing capital growth. Therefore, when determining the risks of recommending QDVO, I should note that the main threat to such an investment is a change in the value of US technology stocks, such as QDVO and JEPQ, which make up the majority of the portfolio. By investing in QDVO, it is worth accepting that, as an investor, your exposure to Nasdaq market risk is almost 100%. In return, however, this gives you the opportunity to earn higher returns, which may be provided by Alpha rather than Beta. This requires the right choice on the part of the portfolio manager. It is up to him to decide whether to “sit it out” and not sell calls, hoping for a rebound, or vice versa. If calls are sold too early for fear of a market correction, growth potential will not be realized. In that case, the return on capital appreciation from JEPQ will be no different.

Conclusion

The results of a comparative analysis of the fundamental differences between QDVO and JEPQ allow us to identify similarities and differences between them. Their most similar criteria are that both funds focus on investing in technology sector stocks (Mag-7) using an option strategy to generate income (rather than relying solely on dividends from the stocks themselves). QDVO and JEPQ pursue a common goal of achieving an ideal combination of income and growth, allowing investors to receive not only high monthly payments, but also to see their capital grow along with the market capitalization of the market.

At the same time, however, fundamental differences between them allow me to make decisions related to recommending “Buy” for QDVO and “Hold” for JEPQ. Despite the fact that I previously recommended buying the latter ETF, my rating revision is solely due to the fact that I no longer wish to use my free capital to buy JEPQ, opting instead for QDVO. That said, if you need maximum current income right now, then I recommend buying JEPQ, since QDVO is more suitable for investors who are focused on realizing the growth potential of their assets in the medium to long term.

data.title: Yield Advantage: Why EM Local Bonds Are Leading DM Bonds In 2025
data.description:
EM local-currency bonds offer high real yields, strong 2025 returns, and resilience to shocks, driven by credible policy and solid fundamentals.

Emerging market (EM) local currency bonds have delivered astounding performance in 2025 despite initial fears around local currency devaluation versus the US dollar due to tariffs. Year-to-date, the asset class outpaced most fixed income segments, including US high yield and the broad US and global markets. EM local bonds are an attractive option for fixed income portfolios, offering high yields from issuers with credible monetary policy in a world where developed markets grapple with sticky inflation and limits on central bank effectiveness. With strong fundamentals and higher yields, the asset class has shown that it is resilient to external risk-off shocks.

Higher Yields, Better Value

Worries about rising inflation and debt levels in the US prompted investors to consider the diversification benefits of non-US dollar assets, raising the appeal for EM local bonds, but that’s only half of the story. On top of that, sovereign bond yields in EM countries remain high, as many EM central banks started tightening in 2021 well ahead of advanced economies, front-loading hikes to contain post-pandemic inflation. The result has been high real yields, which continue to be an important driver of return.

High Yields in EM Cushion Market Turbulence

EM Sovereign Local Bond Returns Exceed Those In Developed Markets YTD

Resilient Markets

Emerging markets have proven to be resilient to external risk-off shocks this year, displaying a significant improvement post the Global Financial Crisis. Even this year, after April’s US tariff tantrum, EM local bonds quickly regained their footing and have led the fixed income category year to date. This isn’t a work of magic but policy-driven: EM policymakers have built credibility through years of disciplined spending and orthodox monetary policy, resulting in more anchored inflation expectations and stronger fundamentals versus developed markets. Further, higher funding in local currency and better debt management have reduced their sensitivity to global/external shocks.

After an outstanding run in 2025, EM local currency bonds have regained investor attention – leading with performance and explained by fundamentals. Strong debt management and responsible monetary policies have provided EMs with room to cut rates, while local yields remain elevated. We believe the high level of income, robust fundamentals, and diversification benefits make emerging markets local currency bonds an attractive addition to a global fixed income portfolio.

data.title: Universal Insurance: I Regret Not Buying Earlier, Now I'm Quite Too Late
data.description:
Three months after my previous analysis, Universal Insurance Holdings, Inc. (UVE) has already risen by 33%. I regret not taking a position despite its robust performance and fundamentals. To be honest, I understand the recent market stance since hurricanes did not make any landfall in the US for the first time since 2015. Valuation remains higher than historical averages, but the market still doesn’t seem to care about it. Technicals reflect it due to solid bullish signals.

The insurance industry in Florida has faced several challenges in the past 12 months. Sticky inflation and claims from Hurricanes Debby, Helene, and Milton are some examples. Yet, it seems that they also opened more opportunities for key players as they further highlighted the necessity of property and casualty, or P&C insurance. Universal Insurance Holdings, Inc., is one of the popular P&C companies in the state that benefited from these trends.

UVE Q3 2025: A Brief Recap Of Its Success

During Q3 2025, its direct premiums earned amounted to $534.1M, up by 5.2% YoY from $507.7M. This shows a stronger demand for its insurance products. As you can see, its policies in force rose by nearly 40,000 units, or 4.7%, while total premiums increased by 4.3%. I think we can attribute it to its effective pricing structure to capture demand and maintain high policyholder retention rates despite rising prices. If we divide premiums by policies, the average value in Q3 2025 was $2,415.73 versus $2,422.99. It was only a difference of about $7 or $8, but it made a huge difference in ensuring a solid client base. With that, it was also able to generate higher commissions and policy fees. Overall, the total revenue reached $401.0M, up by 3.5% YoY from $387.6M.

Moreover, it incurred lower expenses due to two reasons. First, it maintained its efficiency and prudent underwriting strategies. We can see the manageable increase in its policy acquisition costs. Second, no hurricanes made landfall in the US. So, claims and losses decreased. Hence, it enjoyed a huge increase in profits. Its net income margin improved a lot to 9.9% from -4.2% YoY.

Challenges

Inflation is still a challenge for insurance companies. Of course, it's a staple for many people, especially residents and business owners in states along the Atlantic coastline. However, this can still impact purchasing power as well as their capacity to sustain their policy payments. At this point, inflation is back to its 14-month high of 3.0%, so prices are still increasing. This may be exacerbated by new tariffs due to their inflationary nature. Hurricanes are also risk factors. They are the main reason for the existence of many P&C insurers in Florida, but high intensity and frequency can pulverize the margins of many companies. After all, they can lead to massive claims from policyholders. High competition in the state must also be considered. Large players like HCI (HCI), American Coastal (ACIC), and Heritage (HRTG) are present there.

Mitigants/Drivers

Despite these potential challenges, I believe that UVE is still well-positioned due to its strategic and disciplined underwriting policies. It maintains its conservative approach to growth by maintaining an ample portion of premium for ceding to the total direct premiums earned. In Q3 2025, it ceded 32.7% of its premiums earned versus 31.9% YoY. This means that the risk of this portion of premiums is passed on to its reinsurers. The actual amount also increased by nearly 8%. With that, UVE ceded a larger portion of its premiums to limit risks while expanding its operating capacity.

If we look at its Balance Sheet, the combined amount of prepaid reinsurance premiums and reinsurance recoverable comprises 25% of the total assets. This is also more than enough to cover unpaid losses or claims. In addition, it continues to increase its investments, which allows it to multiply its income streams. More interestingly, its cash level keeps expanding. This makes UVE very liquid. The total amount of cash, prepaid reinsurance premiums, investments, and reinsurance recoverable exceeds the total liabilities. And if we look at its equity, retained earnings comprise a huge portion of it. Considering this, UVE maintains a huge capital buffer to sustain its operating capacity and dividends.

Moreover, I like that UVE is expanding to non-Florida states. Premiums, policies, and total insured value rose noticeably on a YoY basis. This allows it to offset its softness in Florida. This may also help it spread out risks instead of relying solely on the state, which is highly exposed to hurricanes. Lastly, it may help increase its brand popularity and domestic market presence.

On a broader view, I believe that the recent interest rate cut may also help it due to several factors. First, lower interest rates can boost economic activity, which may raise employment, wages, and purchasing power. Second, this can make credit cards more affordable, which can support consumer spending on insurance products. Third, this can lower the borrowing costs or interest expense on its variable-rate borrowings.

Valuation

After revisiting its valuation, UVE is still expensive relative to previous years. It is still trading above historical averages, which suggests that the stock price increased more than necessary. P/B, for instance, shows that the stock now trades at 1.84x its book value versus the five-year average of only 1.32x. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP will only be $23.25. The same applies to P/S, which shows that UVE trades at a higher level using sales per share. This suggests a TP of $23.26.

However, what I really failed to accept before is that the market is not always linear. It doesn’t always care whether a stock is cheaper or more expensive than in previous years. As long as book value and/or sales increase, the price trend will most likely follow. That’s what we see in its stock price.

The problem is that the price gap between my previous coverage and my target price has already widened too far. So, downside risks must be considered. It is possible that it may sustain the uptrend. However, hurricanes can easily change the tide.

Technicals

UVE’s technicals are still bullish as the price continues to increase with higher highs and lows. It is also trading above the 50-day and 200-day SMA, showing uptrend continuation. After all, the current price trend is still higher than the moving averages in the past two and twelve trading months. In addition, the 50-day SMA is still trending upwards, which suggests that market sentiments are still optimistic. But I advise investors to exercise some caution due to the selling trend in the past four weeks. They may want to wait for the price to either break below the $30.54 support or not before deciding. That can tell us of its potential direction.

Its MACD and RSI can explain this. The MACD line is moving downwards below the signal line, which shows that selling volume continues to increase. Meanwhile, the histogram is within negative territory, which implies that selling volume has outpaced buying volume recently. Lastly, RSI shows that the increasing selling volume was due to the recent overbuying. Profit-takers are moving, while investors are starting to become cautious as the market reassesses its value. It’s now back to the normal range, so there is now enough buying room. Yet, we may wish to wait for it to reach 30 to become oversold before buying. That can prompt buying volume again, just like what happened last August.

Conclusion

Universal Insurance Holdings, Inc. remains a highly impressive company with its robust growth and margins. This is supported by its prudent and disciplined underwriting strategies, high liquidity, and expansion to other states. Valuation is still high, and the stock appears fully valued, which makes it harder for me to enter no matter how much I want to. Technicals seem to adhere to my view, as the recent overbuying prompts selling volume. I’m reiterating my hold rating on this stock.

data.title: U.S. Bank Stocks Outperform In November
data.description:
Investors favored small- and medium-capitalization US bank stocks following the third-quarter 2025 earnings season.

The 208 banks in an S&P Global Market Intelligence analysis recorded a median total return of 5.4% in November, surpassing the 2.0% gain of the market-cap-weighted S&P US BMI Banks index and the 0.2% gain of the S&P 500. Only seven of the banks had negative monthly returns.

Sixteen banks, 15 of which had market caps of less than $3.5 billion at Nov. 28, posted double-digit percentage gains. Uniondale, New York-based Flushing Financial Corp. (FFIC), with a market cap of $554.3 million at the end of November, led the group with a 20.0% gain.

The median price-to-adjusted tangible book value (TBV) of the banks included in the analysis was 136.8% as of Nov. 28.

Least expensive banks

For the second consecutive month, First Internet Bancorp (INBK) was the least expensive bank based on price-to-adjusted TBV ratio. It ended November priced at 48.6% of adjusted TBV, up from 42.7% at Oct. 31.

Dallas-based First Foundation Inc. (FFWM), the second least expensive bank, announced in October that it had agreed to merge with Denver-based FirstSun Capital Bancorp (FSUN), which ranked 15th on the list.

In an investor presentation, FirstSun projected that the transaction would be 14.3% dilutive to TBV on a GAAP basis, resulting in an earnback period of 3.3 years. On a cash basis, the estimate was a TBV accretion of 7%. FirstSun also disclosed that it plans to restructure First Foundation's balance sheet.

The banking subsidiaries of First Foundation and FirstSun reported notable nondepository financial institution (NDFI) exposure for the third quarter. Loans to one NDFI segment, business credit intermediaries, accounted for 7.7% of First Foundation Bank's gross loans and leases, the ninth highest ratio across the US banking industry. FirstSun unit Sunflower Bank NA reported that its NDFI delinquency ratio spiked 247 basis points, representing the fourth-largest quarter-over-quarter increase among banks with more than $5 billion in total assets at Sept. 30.

Bethesda, Maryland-based Eagle Bancorp Inc. (EGBN), the third least expensive bank, is grappling with credit and management succession issues. It had the fifth-highest monthly return in the analysis at 13.3%.

First Guaranty Bancshares Inc. (FGBI), ranked sixth, was the weakest market performer in November. Its total return for the month was negative 27.0%, which was almost 25 percentage points worse than any other bank in the analysis.

The Hammond, Louisiana-based bank posted a net loss available to common shareholders of $45.6 million for the third quarter, following a $7.9 million net loss in the second quarter and a $6.7 million net loss in the first quarter. First Guaranty's third-quarter results included a $31.3 million sequential increase in the provision for credit losses and a $12.9 million goodwill impairment.

First Guaranty disclosed in its third-quarter Form 10-Q that $39.8 million of the $47.9 million provision was associated with one commercial lease relationship. The company has a $52.0 million credit exposure associated with commercial lease financing to entities related to an auto parts manufacturer that declared Chapter 11 bankruptcy during the third quarter, according to the filing.

The credit exposure consists of one $17.2 million commercial lease, which was past due on its payments and placed on nonaccrual as of Sept. 30, and three commercial leases totaling $34.8 million that were current on payments and remained on accrual status as of Sept. 30. First Guaranty has downgraded all four lease credits to substandard and impaired status.

Most expensive banks

Among the 20 most expensive banks by price-to-adjusted TBV, only The Bancorp Inc. (TBBK) and Wells Fargo & Co. (WFC) recorded negative returns for November. The Bancorp had a return of negative 2.0%, while Wells Fargo logged a return of negative 0.8%.

Still, The Bancorp remained the highest-valued bank for the fifth month in a row. Its price-to-adjusted TBV ratio was 413.8% as of Nov. 28, down 8 percentage points from the end of October but more than 70 percentage points higher than any other bank in the analysis.

Wells Fargo, ranked 13th, reduced outstanding exposure to office and retail commercial real estate (CRE) loans during the third quarter. On the other hand, it added to the institutional and industrial/warehouse CRE categories. Overall, the bank's CRE loans declined 1.7% quarter over quarter, according to a Form 8-K filed Oct. 14.

About this analysis

S&P Global Market Intelligence analyzed US banks trading on the Nasdaq, NYSE or NYSE American with total assets of more than $3 billion. The analysis excludes banks in the mutual holding company ownership structure and other operating subsidiaries.

Adjusted tangible book value is calculated as the sum of tangible common equity, loss reserves and unrealized gain or loss from held-to-maturity securities, tax-adjusted at the 21% corporate rate, less nonperforming assets and loans 90 or more days past due but still accruing interest, divided by common shares outstanding.

Original Post
